coredata.dc:title: One-step green synthesized fluorescent carbon nanodots from bamboo leaves for copper(II) ion detection
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yingshuai
Zhao, Yanan
Zhang, Yuanyuan
coredata.dc:description: High quantum yield carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are successfully synthesized via a simple, low cost, and green hydrothermal method using bamboo leaves as carbon source for the first time. Branched polyethylenimine (BPEI)-capped CQDs (BPEI-CQDs) are prepared by coating the CQDs with BPEI via electrostatic adsorption. The BPEI-CQDs are then employed as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective Cu2+ detection. Experimental results show that the synthesized CQDs have average size of 3.6nm in diameter with narrow size distribution. The biomass-based CQDs offer high quantum yield of 7.1%. The BPEI-CQDs-based sensing system renders a simple, reliable and sensitive Cu2+detection with limit of detection (LOD) as low as 115nM and a dynamic range from 0.333 to 66.6μM. In addition, the BPEI-CQDs are successfully used to detect Cu2+ in river water, demonstrating its good selectivity and great potential for analysis of environmental water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Biomass
Cu2+ detection
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface modification of CQDs with BPEI
PH effect on fluorescence emission of BPEI-CQDs
BPEI-CQDs nanoprobe for Cu2+ detection lab buffers
Validation of application of BPEI-CQDs nanoprobe in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of as-prepared CQDs (inset: size distribution histogram).

(A) Absorption (a) and PL emission (b) spectra of CQDs. Inset: photograph of a CQDs solution under a hand-held UV lamp (365nm). (B) PL emission spectra of the CQDs obtained under excitations from 365 to 525nm with 20nm increase. Inset: normalized PL emission spectra. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
FT-IR spectra of pristine CQDs, BPEI alone and BPEI-CQDs.
Fluorescence intensity change of BPEI-CQDs at different pH.

Fluorescence response of BPEI-CQDs in the presence of different metal ions (performed in PBS solution with pH 4.0 (A), 5.0 (B), 6.0 (C); the concentration of each type of metal ion is 50μM; the fluorescence intensity of BPEI-CQDs is recoded at 450nm in absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. Excitation wavelength is 365nm.).

(A) Emission spectra of BPEI-CQDs in presence of Cu2+ with concentrations ranging from 0 to 140μM in a pH 4 PBS solution. Inset: The plot of F 0/F −1 of BPEI-CQDs at 450nm versus the concentrations of Cu2+. (B) Stern–Volmer plot of Cu2+-induced signal response of BPEI-CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The FL responses of BPEI-CQDs upon adding the as-prepared river water sample and various concentrations of standard Cu2+ solutions.
Processing diagram for the synthesis and application of CQDs.

head.ce:title: One-step green synthesized fluorescent carbon nanodots from bamboo leaves for copper(II) ion detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Clean Energy & Advanced Materials, Southwest University, No. 2 Tiansheng Road, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Clean Energy & Advanced Materials, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials & Energy, Southwest University, No. 2 Tiansheng Road, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials & Energy, Southwest University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute for Clean Energy & Advanced Materials, Southwest University, No. 2 Tiansheng Road, Chongqing 400715, China. Tel.: +86 23 68254943; fax: +86 23 68254969.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High quantum yield carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are successfully synthesized via a simple, low cost, and green hydrothermal method using bamboo leaves as carbon source for the first time. Branched polyethylenimine (BPEI)-capped CQDs (BPEI-CQDs) are prepared by coating the CQDs with BPEI via electrostatic adsorption. The BPEI-CQDs are then employed as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective Cu2+ detection. Experimental results show that the synthesized CQDs have average size of 3.6nm in diameter with narrow size distribution. The biomass-based CQDs offer high quantum yield of 7.1%. The BPEI-CQDs-based sensing system renders a simple, reliable and sensitive Cu2+detection with limit of detection (LOD) as low as 115nM and a dynamic range from 0.333 to 66.6μM. In addition, the BPEI-CQDs are successfully used to detect Cu2+ in river water, demonstrating its good selectivity and great potential for analysis of environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ion (Cu2+), the most abundant essential transition metal ion in human body except zinc and iron ions, plays a very important role in living organism [1]. However, excess dose is highly toxic to organism and Cu2+ is listed as priority pollutant by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [2]. In order to control Cu2+ pollution and Cu2+-induced toxic effect, there is a great demand for simple, green, reliable and sensitive strategy for Cu2+ detection.

Semiconductor quantum dots, the most dominant fluorescent nanoparticles, have been widely used as nanoprobe in sensing [3] and bioimaging [4], due to their unique optical properties. However, The toxicity to human and environment resulting from their heavy metal constituents have attracted more and more concerns [5]. In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as promising photoluminescent nanoparticles. In comparison to conventional inorganic QDs, CQDs exhibit many merits including thermal stability, chemical inertness, good water solubility, and excellent biocompatibility. Thus, CQDs provide great alternatives to semiconductor QDs and organic fluorescent dyes for biosensing and bioimaging [6].

More recently, chitosan, glucose, ethylenediamine-tetraacetic acid salts, citric acid have been carbonized via hydrothermal or microwave-assisted approaches to prepare CQDs [6–9]. Dong et al. synthesized polyamine-capped CQDs by pyrolyzing citric acidvia a 10-cycle procedure. Similarly, citric acid is utilized as a carbon source for microwave-assisted synthesis of CQDs, which are employed as probe for intracellular Cu2+ detection [10]. Alternative to chemical reagents, natural biomass such as sweet potatoes [11] and pomelo peel [12] have been successfully applied as carbon sources for CQDs synthesis, demonstrating a great potential of waste materials recycling. To explore broader carbon sources, in this work natural, low-cost, abundant bamboo leaves are chosen as a carbon source for simple and green synthesis of water soluble, high quantum yield CQDs at first time. Major constituent of bamboo leaves are complex carbohydrate, which contains a large amount of hydroxyls and carboxylic/carbonyl moieties, providing superior carbon source for CQDs preparation. During hydrothermal treatment, the carbohydrate undergoes dehydration, decomposition, and aromatization to form CQDs through nucleation.

Branched polyethyeneimine (BPEI) is a water-soluble cationic polymer, which contains primary, secondary and tertiary amino groups. Since the various amines along the molecular chain provides high-density of positive charges and functional groups, BPEI is widely used in non-viral gene delivery and drug delivery [13,14]. The BPEI is also utilized as water adsorbent and applied for metal ions sensing due to its strong chelation with some heavy metal ions [15]. In this work, BPEI-capped CQDs (BPEI-CQDs) are synthesized by coating the as-prepared CQDs from bamboo leaves with BPEI for selective and sensitive Cu2+ detection. The size and shape of as-prepared CQDs from bamboo leaves are characterized with transmission electron microscope (TEM). Surface coating and luminescence emission of BPEI-CQDs are studied using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), dynamic light scattering (DLS), and fluorescence spectrometer. The optimal operation condition and sensing performance are systematically investigated. A green, simple, reliable, and sensitive sensing method has been developed for the detection of trace Cu2+ in environmental water, such as river water, demonstrating their promising applications in analysis of environmental water.

CuCl2, Co(NO3)2, CaCl2, NiCl2, MnSO4, HgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, BaCl2, CdCl2 and FeCl3, branched polyethylenimine (PEI) (50%, wt%, M
w
=1300) are purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). The phosphate buffered saline (PBS) with different pH values are prepared by adding a concentrated phosphoric acid solution or sodium hydroxide solution into a 0.01M PBS at pH 7.4. Deionized (DI, 18MΩ) water is produced by a water purification system (Q-Grad®1, Millipore) and used in all experiments.

CQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method with fresh bamboo leaves as carbon source. In detail, fresh bamboo leaves are collected and thoroughly washed with tap water and DI water and then dried at room temperature. For cold grinding, a given amount (2.0g) of dried bamboo leaves is cooled and embrittled with liquid nitrogen. The grinding process results in a fine powder, which is dispersed into 25mL of DI water. The resulting mixture is then transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200°C for a period of 6h in an oven. The CQDs solution is obtained after removing the >carbide slag via centrifugation at 8000rpm for 15min. As-prepared dark brown solution is further purified through a 0.22μm membrane filter. CQDs with an average diameter of 3.6nm are obtained. Finally, it is diluted ten times with DI water for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modification of CQDs with BPEI
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, 5mL of 1mgmL−1 BPEI was prepared, to which 5mL of diluted CQDs is dropwise added. The resulting solution is stirred vigorously for 1min using vortex mixer. After 2h incubation at room temperature, cationic polyelectrolyte BPEI is physically adsorbed on CQDs via electrostatic force, resulting in polyamine-capped CQDs. The obtained BPEI-CQDs are employed as fluorescent nanoprobe for Cu2+ detection.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra are examined by an FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo-Fisher, Nicolet 6700). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra are recorded by Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra of as-prepared CQDs are obtained by an FL spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, RF-5301PC). Size and shape of the CQDs are characterized with transmission electronmicroscopy (TEM) images are (JEOL, JEM-2100) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. For preparation of TEM specimens, a given volume of CQDs dispersion is dropped on a carbon-coated copper grid, followed by drying in a vacuum oven for overnight. Zeta potentials of pristine and modified CQDs are measured using a Nano-ZS Zetasizer ZEN3600 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) in DI water.

The dose-dependent response of PEI-CQDs to Cu2+ is investigated. Upon addition of various amount of Cu2+, emission intensity of the BPEI-CQDs at 450nm is recorded under 365nm excitation. Same volume without Cu2+ is applied as negative control. Slit width of 5nm is used for both excitation and emission.

Fig. 2A shows the UV–vis absorption (black line) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra (blue line) of as-prepared CQDs. It is seen that the CQDs exhibits a blue color under UV illumination (365nm) (inset) and displays an excellent aqueous dispersibility. From the CQDs dispersion, an absorption peak centered at 279nm is observed, and a strong PL emission peak centered at 440nm is recorded under excitation at 365nm. Importantly, the as-prepared CQDs have a good stability. The FL intensity maintains more than 81% of fresh prepared CQDs after 10 months storage at 4°C. For long-time storage, CQDs solution can be dried in vacuum oven to obtain their powders, which are redispersed in water when use. There is no observable decrease in the FL intensity (Fig. S1 in Electronic Supplementary information). After PEI coating, same emission intensity with 10nm red-shifts (Fig. S2 in Electronic Supplementary information) is observed as compared with pristine CQDs. 10nm red-shifts should be attributed to BPEI-coating-induced size increase of CQDs. To further investigate optical properties, PL emission spectra of the CQDs are recorded under various excitations. As shown in Fig. 2B, with the increase of excitation wavelength from 365 to 525nm, emission peaks are red-shifted from 440 (blue) to 540nm (green), while the PL intensities are decreased remarkably. BPEI-CQDs also show similar phenomenon. The excitation-dependent emissions are general feature of carbon dots. The mechanism for CQDs luminescence is still not fully understood. Several possibilities are proposed including optical selection of nanoparticles with different size (quantum effect); different distributions of emissive trap sites on each CQD; radiative recombination of excitons; free zigzag sites with a carbene-like triplet ground state [16–18].

Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of as-prepared CQDs is estimated by comparing the wavelength integrated intensity of testing sample to that of a standard. A solution of quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (QY of 54% at 360nm, n =1.33) is used as a standard. The QY is determined by the following equation:
(1)
Q =
Q S
I
I S
A S
A
n 2
n S 2
where Q is the quantum yield, I is the integrated emission intensity, A refers to the absorbance, which is measured by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, and n is refractive index of the solvent. The subscript S denotes the standard value. Specifically, the CQDs is dissolved in DI water (n =1.33) and excited under 360nm. To avoid inner filter effect, the optical density must be below 0.1 at the excitation wavelength. 3nm slit width is used for both excitation and emission. According to above equation, QY of the CQDs is determined to be 7.1% >(Table 1 ), which is comparable to or better than previous reported ones [19–23].

FT-IR spectrometer is employed to characterize PEI capping on CQDs. Fig. 3 shows the FT-IR spectra recorded from pristine CQDs, BPEI-CQDs and PEI alone. In Fig. 3(a), the peaks at 1602 and 1401cm−1 are assigned to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of carboxyl group (COOH) [24], respectively. The peaks at 3422cm−1, 1664cm−1 are ascribed to O–H and C=O stretching vibration. The hydroxyl and carboxylic/carbonyl moieties observed from pristine CQDs are obviously from complex carbohydrates in bamboo leaves. Compared with pristine CQDs, several characteristic peaks of PEI are observed from BPEI-CQDs. The bands at 1565 and 1477cm−1 are attributed to asymmetric and symmetric bending of primary amines (−NH2), respectively, while that at 1324cm−1 is assigned to the stretching of C–N (Fig. 3(c)), demonstrating that branched PEI is successfully capped on CQDs.

Zeta potential of the pristine CQDs is measured as −4.78mV, which is attributed to the negative charged carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on CQDs. It is worth to note that the zeta potential changes to +13.8mV after treatment with positive charged PEI, confirming that PEI is successfully coated on surface of the CQDs via electrostatic force. The amine-rich BPEI is attached on CQDs surface and plays double functions in this system. On one hand, the introduced BPEI brings a high density of positive charges, which make the CQDs high stable in water and various buffers. On the other hand, the abundant amine-nitrogen on BPEI renders high Cu2+ binding capacity. Both of them are of very importance for Cu2+ sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PH effect on fluorescence emission of BPEI-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The luminescence of BPEI-CQDs is investigated under various pH solutions. Fig. 4 shows that the fluorescence intensity of BPEI-CQDs is pH dependent. The fluorescence intensity first increases with a rise of pH from 2.0 to 4.0 and then gradually decreases with a further increase of pH. The highest fluorescence intensity is observed at pH 4.0. This phenomenon is probably ascribed to the different protonation ratio of BPEI at various pH. Under high acidity (pH <4), over-protonation of PEI results in decrease of fluorescence emission. However, at pH >4 the protonation ratio of PEI gradually decreases with the increase of solution pH. Net surface charge could be reduced due to the deprotonation of PEI, resulting in partial aggregation of BPEI-CQDs. Thus, the aggregation further leads to fluorescence decrease as show in Fig. 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BPEI-CQDs nanoprobe for Cu2+ detection lab buffers
ce:section.ce:para: In order to find optimal detection condition, the selectivity of BPEI-CQDs-based Cu2+ sensor is investigated at different pH values. Under acidic conditions (pH ≤3.0), BPEI-CQDs are unable to form complex with Cu2+ because of the over protonation of PEI, while in alkaline solutions (pH >7.0), Cu2+ is unstable due to its hydrolysis [10]. Thus, only pH 4–6 are further evaluated for BPEI-CQDs-based Cu2+ detection. Interference from various metal ions are investigated under pH 4–6 to find optimal pH. As shown in Fig. 5A, only Cu2+ results in significant quenching of the BPEI-CQDs at pH 4.0. Negligible responses are observed from the treatment of other metal ions, including Co2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, and Cd2+. However, Hg2+, Co2+, Ni2+and Fe3+ result in obvious quenching effect on FL emission of the BPEI-CQDs under pH 5.0 (Fig. 5B), while a strong quenching effect is observed under pH 6.0 (Fig. 5C). Considering the highest fluorescence emission and good sensing selectivity, pH 4.0 is chosen as an optimal condition for Cu2+ detection in following experiments.

In addition to selectivity, sensitivity, dynamic range, and LOD are also very important parameters for sensors. To investigate sensitivity of the developed sensor, Cu2+ with concentrations ranging from 0 to 140μM are analyzed by BPEI-CQDs under the optimal condition. Fig. 6A shows that the BPEI-CQDs are sensitive to Cu2+ and the fluorescence intensity at 450nm gradually decreases with the increase of Cu2+ concentration. The quenching effect is ascribed to the inner filter effect [25] of cupric amine complexes [15] formed at the surface of CQDs, which absorbs both excitation(λ =365nm) and emission light of the BPEI–CQDs. To confirm the mechanism, UV–VIS absorbance of BPEI-Cd2+ complex is recorded. Experimental results show that BPEI-Cd2+ complex has no absorption in the wavelength range of BPEI-CQDs’ FL spectra (Fig. S3 in Electronic Supplementary information). It means that the BPEI–Cd2+ complex has no inner filter effect on the FL of the BPEI-CQDs and thus Cd2+ has nearly no interference in the detection of Cu2+.

The quenching effect can be described by the Stern–Volmer equation [26]:
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K S
[ Q ]

where K
S is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, where F 0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities at 450nm in absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively. [Q] represents the concentration of quencher (Cu2+). As shown in Fig. 6A inset, the plot does not fit a linear Stern–Volmer equation in the whole concentration range, but in the low concentration range, the dose-dependent curve is plotted following the Stern–Volmer equation. Fig. 6B shows the plot of F 0/F −1 versus Cu2+ concentration in the range from 0 to 66.6μM. The correlation coefficient (R 2) is 0.999, indicating a very good linear relationship. Based on the results, the detection limit of Cu2+ (3σ/K) is determined to be 115nM, which is much lower than the maximum level (∼20μM) of Cu2+ in drinking water permitted by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [2]. The high sensitivity is mainly attributed to the strong chelation of Cu2+ with BPEI and a high quantum yield of the CQDs.

Interestingly, Fig. 6A shows that the maximum emission peaks of CQDs blue-shift gradually with the increase of Cu2+ concentration. As mentioned in Section 3.1, BPEI coating increases the size of CQDs, then resulting in 10nm red-shifts of the maximum emission peak. With the addition of Cu2+, electrostatically adsorbed BPEI is gradually detached from the CQDs surface due to the strong coordination between BPEI and Cu2+. BPEI detachment results in decrease of CQDs size, in return leading to blue-shift of emission peaks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of application of BPEI-CQDs nanoprobe in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the applicability, real water sample from Jia Ling River (Chongqing, China) is collected and analyzed with our developed BPEI-CQDs-based Cu2+ sensing system. A standard addition method is applied in this detection. In detail, the river water sample is filtered through a 0.22μm membrane to remove particulate matters that may have autofluorescence. River water samples spiked with standard solutions containing Cu2+ from 0 to 10μM are mixed with BPEI-CQDs solutions at pH 4.0. It is seen that the PL intensity decreases with the increase of Cu2+ concentration in standard solution (Fig. 7 ). The developed sensor provides a linear response (R 2 =0.990) to Cu2+ in spiked samples. Despite the interference from numerous minerals and organics in river water, a good linear response to Cu2+ is observed, demonstrating its good selectivity for real sample detection. The result validates that the developed novel BPEI-CQDs is capable of detecting Cu2+ in environmental water samples. Thus, the BPEI-CQDs-based Cu2+ sensing system provides a green, simple, sensitive and reliable method for Cu2+ detection in environmental water.

High quantum yield CQDs are successfully synthesized from bamboo leaves via a hydrothermal method for the first time. The as-prepared CQDs are then capped with BPEI for sensitive and selective Cu2+ detection. Experimental results show that the BPEI-CQDs-based sensing system offers a green, simple, reliable and sensitive Cu2+ detection with LOD as low as 115nM and a dynamic range from 0.333 to 66.6μM. Moreover, river water is successfully analyzed using the developed fluorescent BPEI-CQDs probe, demonstrating its good selectivity and great potential for environmental water sample analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31200604), Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (cstc2012jjA10152), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2012C083), Chongqing Engineering Research Center for Rapid diagnosis of Dread Disease, the start-up grant (SWU111071) from southwest university, and Chongqing Key Natural Science Foundation (cstc2012jjB50011).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2014.02.053.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

Green synthesis of carbon nanodots as an effective fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions

Functional magnetic nanoparticles for non-viral gene delivery and MR imaging

Journal of the American Chemical Society

Synthesis and analytical applications of photoluminescent carbon nanodots

Room temperature and solvothermal green synthesis of self passivated carbon quantum dots
Hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-containing carbon nanodots as the high-efficient sensor for copper(II) ions

Copper ion-selective fluorescent sensor based on the inner filter effect using a spiropyran derivative

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yingshuai Liu completed his doctorate in School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering at Nanyang Technological University (Singapore) in 2011. After a short period post-doctoral training in same group, Dr. Liu joined Institute for Clean Energy and Advanced Materials at Southwest University as an associate professor. His main research interests are nanomaterials synthesis and modification, and nanomaterials-based high-performance biosensors/biochips and their applications in various fields. He has published more than 20 peer-reviewed papers in international journals.

Yanan Zhao received her bachelor degree in chemistry from Anyang Normal University, Henan province, China in July 2012. In September 2012, she was enroled as a master student in Institute for Clean Energy & Advanced Materials, Southwest University, Chongqing, China. Her research work focuses on development and applications of nanomaterials-based optical bio-/chem-sensors.

Yuanyuan Zhang obtained her bachelor degree in chemisty from Yunnan province, China in 2012. She currently is a master student majoring in analytical chemistry at Southwest University, Chongqing, China. Her research interests are nanomaterials-based biosensors/biochips and their applications.
